classification.summary: Clari's demo showcases 11+ features across dashboards, forecasting, deal management, and sales engagement, but lacks a coherent narrative thread connecting them to a single outcome. While the opening promise is 'predictable revenue growth,' the demo rapidly feature-jumps without explaining how each capability builds toward that goal or addressing specific persona needs.
strengths: Clear opening hook: 'Will you meet, beat, or miss your revenue objectives?'
Logical feature grouping (e.g., visibility → forecasting → deal management → engagement)
Consistent brand voice and professional tone throughout
Acknowledges multiple revenue models (subscription, consumption, NDR) showing product depth
Closing CTA ('Schedule a Demo') provides next step
weaknesses: Feature dump: 11 distinct features shown without a connecting red thread
No persona targeting—same narrative regardless of whether viewer is CFO, VP Sales, or rep
Zero credibility proof—no numbers, customer logos, before/after comparisons, or specific metrics
Generic benefit language: 'aligned,' 'confident,' 'informed decisions,' 'nothing slips through the cracks'
No problem-solution-outcome arc—features are listed rather than sequenced to solve a specific pain point
Missing emotional resonance—doesn't speak to role-specific fears (quota miss, visibility gap, deal slippage)
Steps 8–12 feel rushed and interchangeable without clear prioritization
No evidence that these features actually deliver the 'predictable path to growth' promised in step 1
classification.narrative_arc: Opening: 'Meet, beat, or miss revenue targets' → Middle: Rapid progression through visibility (dashboard, real-time insights, pipeline drill-down), consolidation (forecasting, multi-org alignment), deal management (Smart Summaries, engagement, CRM updates), and rep enablement (battlecards, follow-ups, MAPs) → Closing: 'Run Revenue with Clari + CTA.' The middle lacks connective tissue—each feature feels like an item on a product list rather than a step toward solving the opening tension.
classification.persona_targeting: None. The demo uses generic 'your team,' 'your business,' and organizational language that could apply to any buyer. No branching by role (CFO/revenue ops vs. VP Sales vs. rep). No acknowledgment of different stakeholder priorities or success metrics.
classification.proof_elements: None. No specific metrics, customer logos, testimonials, before/after data, or realistic use cases. All claims are aspirational ('AI-driven,' 'unified visibility,' 'confident decision-making') without supporting evidence.
classification.recommendation: Restructure the demo around a single persona's revenue challenge: Choose one buyer (e.g., VP Sales missing forecast accuracy). Open with their specific problem (e.g., 'Pipeline visibility is fragmented across CRM instances—you can't forecast confidently'). Then sequence 4–5 features as steps to solve it (unified forecasting → pipeline inspection → risk detection → team action → verified outcome). Close with a specific metric or customer proof (e.g., 'Customers improve forecast accuracy by 23%' or 'Closed 3 deals faster using Smart Deal Summaries'). This moves from feature dump to outcome-driven story.

steps.tooltip_text: Run Revenue with ClariWill you meet, beat, or miss your revenue objectives? Clari’s Revenue Platform provides a predictable path to growth, identifying revenue leaks early so you can activate your team to address them.Next

Stay on top of progress and stay aligned with Clari’s Dashboard. Track essential metrics, spot risks, and prioritize next steps to keep your team accountable and on track to meet revenue targets.2/13Next
Stay ahead by tracking real-time performance. Clari's AI-driven analytics give insights into KPIs and emerging risks that could impact your business.3/13Next

Discover what's causing changes in your pipeline with real-time insights. View high-level overviews and drill down to specific deals to understand shifts and make informed decisions. Easily inspect pipeline changes for any date range.4/13Next
Forecast all revenue streams with Clari's single, reliable view. Consolidate subscription, consumption, and NDR models for unified visibility and confident decision-making.5/13Next

Achieve clarity and alignment across all CRM and Clari instances with a unified forecasting experience. Easily toggle between orgs, access different views, and provision users enterprise-wide with a single login.6/13Next

Achieve next-level pipeline management with Clari’s AI-powered Smart Deal Summaries. Get a clear, actionable view of every deal, backed by insights that highlight risks, prioritize next steps, and drive informed decisions—so nothing slips through the cracks.7/13Next

Take immediate action the moment you spot risk. With Clari’s sales engagement solution, you can assign tasks directly within Inspect to keep your team aligned, focused, and ready to drive revenue where it matters most.8/13Next

Capture every detail with Clari's Smart CRM Updates. Our AI extracts, tracks, and stores critical information from every conversation, automatically populating CRM fields aligned to your team's needs.9/13Next
Ensure every opportunity is nurtured with Clari's Smart Follow-Up Emails. Keep prospects engaged and reps focused on strategic actions for high-value account engagement.10/13Next

Boost rep confidence and keep deals on track with Clari's Smart Battlecards. AI-triggered keywords provide live objection handling, pricing guidance, and competitive insights on every call. No manager needed.11/13Next
Use Clari's Mutual Action Plans (MAPs) to align sales and customer expectations, track progress, and ensure a clear path to close for predictable revenue.12/13Next
Run Revenue with ClariSchedule a Demo

classification.summary: The demo spans 17 steps covering two main product areas: Analytics/Reports and Publishing/Collaboration. While it includes some credibility markers (G2 rating, IDC leader status, processing 1B+ daily messages), the narrative lacks a clear through-line connecting features to a single outcome. Features are presented sequentially without explaining how they work together to solve a specific problem.
strengths: Opens with credibility markers (G2 #1, IDC leader, 1B+ daily messages processed)
Attempts to cluster features into two logical buckets (Analytics and Publishing)
Includes some outcome-oriented language ('prove ROI', 'align social performance with business goals', 'drive more impact')
Copy acknowledges practical pain points (building reports from scratch, collaboration friction, approval workflows)
Mentions no-login-required sharing, which addresses a real user need
weaknesses: 17 steps is too long and feels like a feature tour rather than a guided story
No clear problem statement—doesn't articulate why someone should care before showing features
Features are listed sequentially without explaining how they combine to create a single outcome
Generic language like 'deeper insights', 'track progress', 'optimize'—vague rather than specific
No persona targeting—doesn't distinguish between social manager, director, executive, or other roles
No proof of customer impact beyond logos/ratings (no case study, metric improvements, or before/after)
Weak connective tissue between Analytics and Publishing sections—why are these shown together?
Copy is functional but lacks emotion—doesn't acknowledge specific stakeholder pain or success metrics
Steps 7-10 and 13-16 feel repetitive (drilling into sub-features without clear value added)
classification.narrative_arc: Opening hook: 'Easily drive more impact from social media' + credibility (G2, IDC). Journey: Rapid tour of Analytics (reports, metrics, customization, filtering) then Publishing (calendars, collaboration, workflows). Closing: Implied but weak—no summary of how these features work together to achieve the opening promise. The story lacks tension, resolution, and emotional resonance.
classification.persona_targeting: None. The demo uses one-size-fits-all language with no role selection or branching. Sprout Social serves multiple personas (social managers, directors, executives, marketers, creative teams), but the demo doesn't differentiate value by role. A social manager cares about speed and collaboration; an executive cares about ROI and alignment with business goals. The demo tries to speak to everyone and thus speaks to no one effectively.
classification.recommendation: Restructure the demo around a single persona and outcome. Choose one: either 'Help a social manager move faster with collaborative publishing' or 'Help an executive prove social ROI with better analytics.' Cut to 8-10 steps max. Open with a specific problem (e.g., 'Our team spends 5 hours building reports each week' or 'Approval workflows are slowing our content calendar'), show 3-4 features that directly solve it, and close with a before/after metric or customer quote. Remove feature sub-drills (e.g., steps 7-10 on report customization) unless they're critical to the core outcome.

steps.tooltip_text: Easily drive more impact from social mediaAdvanced analytics, AI and automation‌ and personalized engagement—there's a reason Sprout has been rated #1 best software on G2 and a leader by IDC. Explore how our comprehensive platform helps teams move faster.See how it works
Get up and running quicklyOur easy-to-navigate interface lets you spend more time driving results. Next
First, let's check out Reports to measure content performance.Next

Quantify your impact in secondsSprout’s Analytics processes 1B+ daily messages, giving you deeper insights, custom metrics‌ and reports to prove ROI and align social performance with business goals.Next
Now, let's take a look at Profile Performance Reports!Next
Showcase data in 20+ reports. Tailor metrics to fit your goals or build customized reports to highlight exactly what matters most to your strategy. Next
Quickly adjust date ranges and comparison periods to uncover insights and track progress with precision.Next
Let’s check out Post Performance Reports.Next
Analyze all posts (even native) to identify top-performing content by impressions, engagement‌ or other key metrics. Compare organic and paid performance to uncover trends and optimize.Next
No more building reports from scratch! Quickly create tailored reports and share with anyone who needs to see them—no Sprout login required. Next
Now, let’s dive into Publishing! Next

Transform your content calendar into a hub for collaboration without frictionPlan and schedule your content faster thanks to customizable approval workflows, shareable links (no login required) and calendar notes that empower teams to strategize quickly.Next
Click “Week” at the top right of your screen to get an even more detailed view. Next

Gain a clear view of your publishing strategy. Filter by tags, campaigns and post types. Track scheduled posts for campaigns and drag-and-drop to adjust the date. Collaborate directly on individual posts.Next
Chat and comment directly on posts, easily share updates and provide feedback to keep everyone in sync. Next
Effortlessly refine and repurpose your best-performing content. Duplicate posts and bring them into the Compose Window, to collaborate, iterate‌ and optimize.Next

classification.summary: This demo consists of a single step with no tooltip text or guidance provided. There is no narrative structure, no feature explanation, no value proposition articulated, and no evidence of any storytelling framework.
strengths: Extremely minimal — no clutter or feature overload
weaknesses: No storytelling whatsoever — completely empty tooltip
No Logic: Zero features explained or connected to any outcome
No Emotion: No persona targeting, no acknowledgment of user goals or pain points
No Credibility: No proof, data, numbers, or customer evidence
No narrative flow or guided experience
Fails to answer any of the three core storytelling questions
Single step with no guidance makes this essentially non-functional as a demo
classification.narrative_arc: There is no narrative arc. The demo does not open, develop, or close. It is a placeholder with zero content.
classification.persona_targeting: None. There is no indication of a target role or persona.
classification.recommendation: Start from scratch. Define a single clear outcome (e.g., 'Empower financial professionals to identify breaking market opportunities 3x faster'). Identify one primary persona (e.g., equity analyst, portfolio manager). Then map 5-8 key features that ladder up to that outcome, with specific proof points (numbers, customer logos, before/after metrics). Build the tooltip copy to guide users through a problem → solution → value journey specific to that role.

classification.summary: The demo showcases SentinelOne's Offensive Security Engine and Secret Scanning capabilities by walking through finding and verifying exploit paths in cloud infrastructure. While it includes concrete evidence (simulated attacks, actual credentials, curl commands), the narrative jumps between features without a clear connecting thread, and there's no persona-specific messaging or acknowledgment of who should care most about this value.
strengths: Strong credibility elements: Shows real simulated attack evidence (curl commands, actual credentials extracted, RCE proof)
Concrete proof methodology: Demonstrates how vulnerabilities are verified through safe attack simulation
Clear evidence visualization: Graph view and multi-angle exploration (public exposures, exploits, misconfigurations)
Specific technical depth: Names exact attack vectors (SSRF, GeoJSON API, metadata services URI paths)
Logical information hierarchy: Starts with critical issues, filters by severity, then explores in detail
weaknesses: Feature dump structure: Transitions from Offensive Security Engine → Secret Scanning (step 19) feel disconnected; no unified outcome
Weak emotional targeting: No mention of who should care (CISO? DevOps? Security ops?), no role-specific language
Generic value statements: 'This is really powerful' and 'helps you know with certainty' lack specificity
No business impact quantified: Steps 5 and 19 mention 'business Impact' and '750+ secret types' but don't quantify consequences or improvements
Abrupt feature introduction: Secret Scanning (step 19) feels tacked on with no connection to prior exploit path narrative
Missing red thread: Features don't clearly ladder to a single long-term outcome (Is it 'reduce risk'? 'Speed remediation'? 'Prove exploitability'?)
No customer proof or benchmarks: No logos, metrics, or before/after comparisons
classification.narrative_arc: Opening hook (step 1): 'Verified Exploit Paths' promise. Middle journey (steps 2-18): Walk through finding critical exploits, viewing evidence, visualizing paths, exploring misconfigurations. Closing (step 19): Abrupt pivot to Secret Scanning with minimal transition. The arc breaks down because Secret Scanning feels like a second feature demo rather than a continuation of the core story.
classification.persona_targeting: Completely generic. No persona selected or mentioned. Language is broad enough for any security role but specific enough for none. Missing opportunities to address: 'As a CISO, you need to prove exploitability to executives' or 'As a DevOps engineer, you need to know what to patch first.' Same narrative applies to all.
proof_elements: Simulated attack execution showing RCE is possible (step 7)
Actual curl command demonstrating SSRF exploitation (step 11)
Real credentials extracted via metadata services (step 11)
Multi-factor evidence: public exposures + exploits + misconfigurations (steps 14-18)
Quantified secret detection: '750+ types of secrets' (step 19)
classification.recommendation: Restructure the demo around a single persona and outcome. Example: 'As a Cloud Security Lead, reduce mean-time-to-remediation by prioritizing verified exploits over false positives.' Then show: (1) How Offensive Security Engine prioritizes exploits with proof of exploitability, (2) How Secret Scanning prevents credential exposure in the same stack, (3) Business impact (e.g., '3x faster remediation vs. static SAST'). This unifies both features under one value promise and makes the narrative coherent. Add one customer metric at the end (e.g., 'Acme Corp reduced findings by 60% in 30 days') to strengthen credibility.

steps.tooltip_text: Product TourAgentless CNAPP with a unique Offensive Security Engine that uses an attacker's mindset to reveal evidence-backed Verified Exploit PathsGet started
Offensive Security EngineTo prioritize findings and verify attack paths, click Offensive Security Engine.This service simulates attacks safely to prove what needs to be resolved to quickly.Next
Let's filter this down to Critical Severity issues, to prioritize findings.Next
We have 2 critical severity Exploits found.Click on a specific Issue to see its detailsNext

The details view includes a Description of the issue, its typical business Impact if left unresolved, as well as Recommended Actions to resolve it.You might think, "This looks familiar... I thought this was patched? Is this really still present?"Next
Click on View Evidence to see how this exploit was found and verifiedNext
Here you can see the simulated attack executed safely, proving that remote code execution is still possible on this instance.Next
Let's go back and explore this other issue found...Next
In this issue description we see info about an exploit using GeoJSON API that can allow access to cloud variables and privilege escalation in a workload/provider.Next
Let's click into View Evidence to see the test performedNext

Here we can see that a curl command was able to exploit the SSRF vulnerability and allow an external attacker to access credentials via a metadata services URI path.We can see the exact credentials that were taken below. Next
We can also visualize the Verified Exploit Paths(TM) by viewing on the GraphNext
We can see these two Critical exploit paths that were simulated.Click into this nginx Amazon EC2 instance to view more detailsNext
We can see all public exposures for this EC2 instance...Next
... available exploits, ...Next
...and misconfigurations.Next
Let's click back over to the Graph Explorer and update it to also show misconfigurations...Next
Now we can see the Critical Verified Exploit Paths and misconfigurations that need to be fixed now!Next

Secret ScanningTo identify leakage of over 750+ types of secrets such as Access Keys and RDS credentials, click Secret Scanning on the left navigation panel.Secret Scanning helps you know with certainty if and where secrets are being leaked.Next

classification.summary: This demo consists of a single step with no tooltip text or guidance provided. There is no narrative structure, no feature explanation, no value proposition articulated, and no credibility evidence presented.
strengths: The demo loads and is accessible (no gating)
weaknesses: Missing tooltip text entirely — no guidance or storytelling at all
Single step with no narrative arc or progression
No features are explained or connected to outcomes
No persona targeting or role-specific messaging
No credibility proof, evidence, or metrics provided
No clear value proposition communicated
Feels like an incomplete or broken demo experience
classification.narrative_arc: No narrative arc exists. There is no opening hook, no middle journey, and no closing value statement.
classification.persona_targeting: Not targeted to any specific persona. No acknowledgement of role, goals, or success metrics.
classification.proof_elements: None. No credibility elements are present.
classification.recommendation: Rebuild this demo from scratch with a clear narrative. Start by defining: (1) What single outcome does Pearson's product create? (2) Who is the target persona watching this? (3) What proof can you show? Then structure 5-10 steps that guide the viewer through a problem, show how the product solves it, and close with specific evidence of value. Add meaningful tooltip text to each step that connects features to the outcome.

classification.summary: This demo tells a cohesive story about moving from detecting standing privileges to eliminating them through CyberArk and Wiz integration. It follows a clear problem → detection → remediation → validation arc, showing how excessive cloud access is identified, flagged, secured through policy application, and then verified in real-world use.
strengths: Clear problem statement upfront: 'detect standing privileges and move to zero standing privileges'
Strong narrative arc with clear stages: Identify → Risk Alert → Remediate → Validate → Success
Specific technical details (e.g., 'Data Scientist role', 'AWS Identity Center', 'BD Tech group') ground the story in reality
Concrete proof elements: Shows actual risk identification, policy creation, session controls (session control ✅, session monitoring ✅, session protection ✅), and permission verification
Features logically ladder to one outcome: each step connects to reducing standing privileges
Strong copy quality with emojis and excitement markers (🚨, ✨, ⚡, ✅, 🔒, 🎉) that guide emotional engagement
Before/after comparison: Shows the risk state, then the secured state with specific permission restrictions
Realistic scenario: includes MFA mention, permission set details, and realistic access constraints
weaknesses: Some steps have no tooltip text (steps 5, 6, 12, 24), creating minor gaps in guidance
Emotion score slightly lower because messaging is generic 'cloud teams' rather than explicitly persona-targeted (though the technical depth implies security/identity teams)
Could be more explicit about which persona cares (Security Engineer? Cloud Architect? Identity Admin?)
No customer logos, testimonials, or external validation mentioned—all proof is from the product itself
Doesn't quantify impact (e.g., 'reduce access risk by X%', 'remediation time cut from X to Y')
classification.narrative_arc: **Opening Hook**: 'Explore how to detect standing privileges and move to zero standing privileges.' **Middle Journey**: Identifies excessive privileges in Wiz → flags risk → sends to CyberArk → applies access controls → user authenticates with MFA → connects to secure role → validates restricted permissions. **Closing**: Celebrates successful policy creation and frames outcome as 'identity security at the scale and speed of your cloud development.'
classification.persona_targeting: Implicitly targeted to Cloud Security Engineers / Identity & Access Management teams. Copy references technical concepts (permission sets, AWS Identity Center, multi-factor authentication, session controls) and security-specific outcomes (zero standing privileges, access policies), but doesn't explicitly address the persona by name or role-specific pain point.
proof_elements: Real risk detection: Shows specific user accounts/groups flagged with excessive privileges
Technical validation: Session control ✅, session monitoring ✅, session protection ✅ checkmarks
Behavioral proof: Logs in as test user, demonstrates MFA requirement, shows restricted permissions (no access to AWS Health/Cost data)
Integration proof: Shows policy successfully created in Wiz platform after CyberArk action
Realistic constraint: Verifies that role is dynamically provisioned/deprovisioned upon connection
classification.recommendation: Add explicit persona targeting and quantified impact. Modify Step 1 to say: 'In this tour, we show Security Engineers and Cloud Architects how to reduce standing privileges from [X%] to near-zero in multi-cloud environments using CyberArk and Wiz—cutting remediation time from days to minutes.' This anchors the story to a specific role's success metric and adds credibility through specificity.

steps.tooltip_text: Secure Cloud Access with CyberArk and WizIn this tour, we're going to explore how to detect standing privileges and move to zero standing privileges. Let's go!Made with  Storylane
Let's begin our journey to zero standing privileges in the Wiz platform by identifying which standing privileges exist in our multi-cloud environment. Next
Navigate to the relevant access control area to check for misconfigurations or excessive access...Next
🚨Risk Identified!Thanks to Wiz's deep visibility, when you scroll down you can see several user accounts and groups with excessive privileges.Next
Let's dive into this Identity Center Group with excessive privileges in the AWS console.
We can see here that the data scientist role associated with the group BD Tech is flagged as a risk for excessive privileges. Time to secure it with CyberArk Secure Cloud Access.Next

Leveraging the CyberArk and Wiz integration, you can now send this issue directly to the CyberArk platform to secure these identities by setting up access policies with CyberArk Secure Cloud Access.Run the action to see the magic ✨
⚡Action executedNow that the issue has been sent for remediation, CyberArk identity security controls will be applied to any user in the BD Tech group when accessing the "Data Scientist" role.Next
Did it work, though?Let's check. Next
We've logged in as a user from the BD Tech Group (after completing strong multi-factor authentication, of course).
Now we'll connect to AWS Identity Center via Secure Cloud Access.
✅ Session control✅ Session monitoring✅ Session protectionActivating...
Locate the permission set for which the secure cloud access policy was recently created by Wiz. Then, hover and click "connect".
By clicking connect, the data scientist role will be dynamically provisioned and deprovisioned upon this connection, ensuring zero standing privileges when connected to the AWS Identity Center.Next
When connected to the AWS Identity Center, we can see that the user can assume the data scientist role.
🔒 Access SecuredThe user's privileges have been escalated to the Data Scientist role, and, as you can see, the user has no permissions to services like AWS Health and Cost data.Next
Meanwhile, back in the Wiz platform...As you can see, the Secure Cloud Access policy was successfully created 🎉Next
And that is identity security–at the scale and speed of your cloud development.Download now

classification.summary: This is a self-guided exploration demo with minimal narrative structure. Step 1 provides a welcome message and explains clickable elements, while Step 2 appears to be either empty or missing tooltip text. There is no clear problem statement, outcome promise, or storytelling arc.
strengths: Clear explanation of interactive mechanics (blue = clickable)
Acknowledges that examples are included (incident and remediation)
Positions Huntress Managed SIEM as novel/early-access
weaknesses: No clear problem or value proposition stated upfront
No persona targeting or role-specific messaging
Step 2 tooltip is completely empty or missing
No specific proof, metrics, customer evidence, or credibility elements
Minimal copy guidance — feels more like a navigation prompt than a story
No features connected to a single outcome
Generic language ('real-world incident') without specificity
No narrative arc: problem → solution → value
Relies entirely on self-guided exploration rather than guided storytelling
classification.narrative_arc: Opening: Generic welcome with navigation instructions. Middle: Implied exploration of detection, reporting, and incident/remediation examples. Closing: None — the story ends with the call to action to explore.
classification.persona_targeting: None. The demo is entirely generic. Uses 'you' broadly without distinguishing between IT ops, security teams, CISOs, or other roles. No acknowledgement of different stakeholder concerns.
classification.proof_elements: None present. No customer logos, testimonials, metrics, before/after data, or specific performance claims. 'Real-world incident' is mentioned but not substantiated.
classification.recommendation: Rewrite the opening to include a clear hook that answers: 'What problem does Huntress solve?' and 'Why should I care?' Example: 'Huntress detects threats that slip past your firewall. See how [ROLE] found a breach in minutes, not days.' Then structure the clickable elements as a guided path (not free exploration) that builds from threat detection → investigation → remediation, with at least one real metric or customer proof point embedded.

steps.tooltip_text: Welcome to the Huntress Demo!Feel free to click around this self-guided demo; anything highlighted in blue is clickable:Clicking on product icons gives you insight into what Huntress detects and reportsThe incident and remediation examples give you an idea of what you would see in a real-world incidentBe one of the first to see Huntress Managed SIEM in actionNext

classification.summary: Outreach's demo walks through 16 features spanning account management, sequencing, task prioritization, call coaching, and manager analytics. While each feature is described, they lack a connecting thread—the demo feels like a capability showcase rather than a cohesive story about solving a specific sales problem and driving measurable outcomes.
strengths: Opens with a clear intent statement: 'grow, organize, and manage their book of business & find every qualified opportunity'
Covers both seller and manager workflows, showing breadth of audience
Uses specific UI references (Smart View, Smart Account Plans, 360 View, Content Cards, Sentiment Analysis) which feel concrete
Logical sequence flow: account targeting → planning → prospecting → execution → coaching → analytics
weaknesses: 16 steps is too long and dilutes impact—no feature clustering or prioritization
No specific numerical claims or evidence (no 'increase reply rates by X%', 'reduce time spent by Y%', etc.)
Generic language throughout: 'help teams grow', 'maximize reply rates', 'increase success rate'—these are promises, not proofs
No customer logos, testimonials, or before/after comparisons
Persona messaging is split between sellers and managers (steps 1-10 vs 11-15) but neither path is deeply persona-targeted—uses generic stakeholder language
No acknowledgment of specific seller pain points (e.g., pipeline shortfall, low reply rates, manual data entry) or manager challenges (e.g., coaching at scale, identifying top performers)
Missing a single, compelling outcome—instead lists many things the product does
Copy lacks specificity: 'Coach sellers to reach out to your highest value prospects' doesn't explain the concrete impact
classification.narrative_arc: Opening: 'Outreach helps teams grow and manage their book of business.' Middle: Feature march—account identification → planning → sequencing → task management → call coaching → meeting booking → manager reporting → sentiment analysis. Closing: None—demo ends abruptly with no summary of value or call to action. The arc lacks a problem hook and a payoff moment.
classification.persona_targeting: Weakly targeted to two personas (seller and manager) but without differentiation. Sellers see prospecting features (steps 1-10), managers see reporting/coaching (steps 11-15). However, there's no role-specific messaging about their unique pain points, success metrics, or risks. Copy remains generic for both personas.
classification.proof_elements: None. No numbers, no customer proof, no before/after comparisons, no testimonials, no logos. Descriptions are feature-focused and vague ('maximize reply rates', 'increase success rate', 'help you execute tasks efficiently').
classification.recommendation: Narrow the demo to 5-7 steps and anchor it to ONE measurable outcome: 'Increase qualified pipeline meetings by X% in Y weeks.' Cluster features under that outcome (e.g., Account Targeting + Sequencing = More Qualified Prospects; Task Prioritization + Call Coaching = Higher Close Rates; Manager Analytics = Better Coaching Decisions). Add one specific proof point—e.g., 'customers using Outreach's sequencing + sentiment analysis increase first-response rates by 34%' or a customer testimonial from a sales leader. Finish with a clear value summary tied back to the opening promise.

steps.tooltip_text: Prospect & Manage AccountsIn this tour, we’ll walk through how Outreach helps teams grow, organize, and manage their book of business & find every qualified opportunity.1/16NextMade with  Storylane
Identify & prioritize accounts and contacts with similar attributes to your target customers to execute a personalized prospecting strategy at scale.2/16Next

Right from an Account Smart View, you can identify accounts that need more attention and drill in to build a prospecting plan. Here you can see it’s probably time to send Dell an email based on a lack of activity.3/16Next
Using Smart Account Plans, your account team can build an account based sales strategy with visibility across prospecting and account management teams.4/16Next
Use sequences tailored to your various target audiences to ensure you’re reaching the right people with the most effective message. 5/16Next
Edit sequence content and steps based on what you know about your prospect to ensure you personalize your message and maximize reply rates.6/16Next

Outreach’s 360 View helps you execute tasks efficiently and effectively by prioritizing high-impact activities, such as notifying you of new inbound leads or surfacing tasks with leads and contacts likely to convert to the top of your list.7/16Next
Automated progression of tasks help sellers engage more efficiently to generate qualified pipeline.8/16Next
When you’re on a call with a prospect, live Content Cards use AI to coach in real-time and help you increase your success rate in booking meetings.9/16Next
And when a prospect agrees to next steps, you can generate a meeting in one click. 10/16Next
For managers, Outreach helps you focus your sellers on activities that are most effective for generating pipeline.11/16Next
In the Team Performance report, you can review prospects contacted to identify trends and coach sellers to reach out to your highest value prospects.12/16Next
Additionally, you can see trends in email sentiment to coach pipeline-generating teams to reach the right personas with the right message.13/16Next
Outreach’s Sentiment Analysis groups responses and objections so you can coach your team on follow-up, messaging, disqualification, and targeting.14/16Next
Managers and RevOps teams can analyze sequence content and build successful campaigns around messages that are driving the most meetings.15/16Next
